coredata.dc:title: Fe3+ assisted water-soluble nickel nanocluster for the “on-off-on” fluorescence determination of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiaqing
Yao, Nan
Zhang, Xiwen
Liu, Yueying
coredata.dc:description: An “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor has been constructed to determine ascorbic acid (AA) based on water-soluble glutathione-decorated nickel nanoclusters (NiNCs) with assistance of trivalent iron ions (Fe3+). The fluorescence could be effectively quenched (off) upon adding Fe3+ due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and the internal filtering effect (IFE). Meanwhile, the fluorescence of NiNCs–Fe3+ systems could be again recovered (on) after introducing AA, which is attributed for the redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA. The sensor can detect AA in the linear range of 20–200 μmol/L with a LOD of 7.45 μmol/L. More importantly, the proposed approach has been successfully applied for the detection of AA content in commercially available tablets and beverages as well as in common fruits with satisfactory recovery ranges, suggesting the potential sensing platform for AA detection. Therefore, the prepared sensor offers distinct advantages including low cost, simple operation, good sensitivity, and high selectivity, which has a broad application prospect of metal nanoclusters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nickel nanoclusters
Fluorescence detection
Trivalent iron ions
Ascorbic acid
On-off-on fluorescent switch

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay of AA in real samples
Design strategy of NiNCs/Fe3+ fluorescent sensor for the detection of AA
Sensitivity and selectivity testing for AA detection
Determination of AA content in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21775087 ) and Scientific Research Project of Beijing Educational Committee (Grant No. KM202010028007 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV-absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of NiNCs. Inset: photographs of NiNCs under natural light (a) and 365 nm UV lamp (b). (B) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 358 nm.
(A) TEM and HRTEM images of NiNCs. (B) Size distribution histogram of NiNCs in ultrapure water. (C) FT-IR spectra of GSH and NiNCs. (D) XPS spectra of Ni 2p.
The full ESI-MS spectrum of NiNCs in negative mode. (Inset: the experiment and simulated isotopic patterns of [Ni3(SG)4-H]-.
(A) Corresponding fluorescence intensity of NiNCs with the different concentrations of NaCl. (B) Stability of NiNCs for one months of storage at 4 °C.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of NiNCs (a), NiNCs/Fe3+ (b), and NiNCs/Fe3+/AA (c). Inset: their corresponding photographs after corresponding photographs after excitation under a 365 nm UV analyzer. TEM images of NiNCs/Fe3+ (B) and NiNCs/Fe3+/AA (C). (D) The practical size distribution of NiNCs, NiNCs/Fe3+, and NiNCs/Fe3+/AA. (E) Absorption spectra of Fe3+, exaction and emission spectra of NiNCs. (F) Time-resolved fluorescence lifetime analysis of NiNCs, NiNCs/Fe3+, and NiNCs/Fe3+/AA.

(A) QE values for NiNCs/Fe3+ at different pH. (B) QE values without AA and RE values with AA in the different concentrations of Fe3+. (C) Fluorescence intensity of NiNCs/Fe3+, NiNCs/Fe3+/AA at different time of incubation.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of after reacting with various concentrations of AA from 0–1000 μM. (B) Fluorescence recovery (F/F0) plotted against AA concentration. (C) Linear relationship between F/F0 and CAA from 20–200 μM. (D) The selectivity of NiNCs/Fe3+ system toward AA. (The concentrations of all substances were 5 times higher than those of AA, except Cys and Lys, which were 1times higher than those of AA).

Determined (red) and reported (black) AA contents in three different kinds of vitamin C tablets and two different vitamin beverages. The value represents the average of three independent measurements.
Schematic diagram of the on-off-on fluorescent strategy for the detection of AA detection based on NiNCs adjusted by Fe3+ as a bridge.
Determine the AA content in three different vitamin C tablets, two different vitamin beverages and two different fresh fruits.

head.ce:title: Fe3+ assisted water-soluble nickel nanocluster for the “on-off-on” fluorescence determination of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing, 100048, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing, 100048, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor has been constructed to determine ascorbic acid (AA) based on water-soluble glutathione-decorated nickel nanoclusters (NiNCs) with assistance of trivalent iron ions (Fe3+). The fluorescence could be effectively quenched (off) upon adding Fe3+ due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and the internal filtering effect (IFE). Meanwhile, the fluorescence of NiNCs–Fe3+ systems could be again recovered (on) after introducing AA, which is attributed for the redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA. The sensor can detect AA in the linear range of 20–200 μmol/L with a LOD of 7.45 μmol/L. More importantly, the proposed approach has been successfully applied for the detection of AA content in commercially available tablets and beverages as well as in common fruits with satisfactory recovery ranges, suggesting the potential sensing platform for AA detection. Therefore, the prepared sensor offers distinct advantages including low cost, simple operation, good sensitivity, and high selectivity, which has a broad application prospect of metal nanoclusters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA or vitamin C) is an important vitamin in the human body. Since the human body cannot directly synthesize AA [1], it needs to be obtained from food or drugs. Among them, obtaining AA from fruits is a simple and healthy way, and the content of AA has become one of the most significant indicators for evaluating the nutritional qualities of fruits [2]. As a micronutrient, AA has a strong antioxidant capacity [3], which can scavengers free radicals and prevent diseases such as cancer [4], cardiovascular disease [5], and Parkinson's disease [6]. In addition, AA is also essential for promoting protein synthesis [7], maintaining vitamin E levels [8], and iron absorption [9]. Lack of AA may lead to a variety of diseases, including colds, scurvy, and cancer [10]. Nowadays, with the deepening of research on AA, more functions of AA have been discovered. AA is also used as a food additive to prevent food deterioration and improve food quality [11], and it has also been added to health care products and cosmeceuticals [12]. Therefore, accurate analysis of AA levels is important for monitoring the quality of pharmaceuticals, beverages, and fresh fruit.

So far, there are many methods to analyze AA, including chromatography [13], iodometric titration [14], electrochemical method [15], colorimetric method [16] and fluorescence analysis [17]. Among them, fluorescence analysis has become a hot research topic because of its simple operation, high sensitivity, and low cost. Usually, the fluorescence comes from the luminescence platforms such as quantum dots [18,19], luminescent nanoparticles [20], organic fluorescent dyes [21], and metal nanoclusters [22]. Fluorescent metal nanoclusters exhibit outstanding light fastness, the larger Stokes shift, hypotoxicity, and favorable biocompatibility compared to other fluorescent materials such as organic dyes and quantum dot, which makes them potentially promising for applications in bio-sensing, imaging, and catalysis [23–25]. Currently, extensive research is focused on gold [26], silver [24], and copper [27–29]nanoclusters. Although copper is also a non-precious metal with low price, the poor stability of copper nanoclusters greatly limits its practical application [30]. Notably, NiNCs exhibit better stability and cost low. However, there are fewer studies about the preparation of fluorescent NiNCs and their application in sensing was limited by the difficulty of preparing stable and extremely microminiature particles [31].

In order to obtain higher analytical sensitivity and immunity to interference, fluorophores or dyes were combined with quenchers such as MnO4 − [32], MnO2 [33], Cr2O7 2− [34], CrO4 2− [35], HClO/ClO− [36], Cu2+ [37], Fe3+ [38], and Hg2+ [39] in the exist of analytes to establish new “on-off-on” regulable fluorescence sensor. The original fluorescence of the fluorophore or dye is quenched by the quencher (from “on” to “off”), and then the fluorescence of the system is restored by the reaction between the AA and the quencher (from “off” to “on”) [40]. Compared with the traditional “on-off” concentration-dependent fluorescence sensing, the “on-off-on” indirect fluorescence sensor has higher analytical sensitivity and has been widely used in the analysis of bio-small molecules, environmental pollutants, food contaminants, and pharmaceuticals [41–43]. As compared with the heavy metal ion quenchers that will be commonly used in sensors, Fe3+ is widely used with “on-off-on” fluorescent chemosensor because of its innocuity and environmental protection advantages [44]. As far as we know, this is the first study to combine nickel nanoclusters with Fe3+ to create an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for the detection of AA.

Herein, we have developed an “on-off-on” fluorescent method based on NiNCs regulated by Fe3+ for AA detection (Scheme 1 ). NiNCs are prepared using glutathione GSH as a template and AA as a reducing agent. Noticeably, we have found that Fe3+ results in the fluorescence decrease of metal nanoclusters owing to combining ACQ with IFE behavior between Fe3+ and NiNCs. Moreover, Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+ when AA is introduced into the NiNCs/Fe3+ sensing system, and NiNCs fluorescence is restored. Therefore, combining nickel nanoclusters with Fe3+ is used to establish a new selective and sensitive “on-off-on-off” fluorescent approach for the detection of AA.

Nickel nitrate hexahydrate solid, sodium hydroxide solid, fructose (Fru), maltose (Malt), and glucose (Glu) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Reduced glutathione was purchased from McLean (Shanghai, China). Serine (Ser), ascorbic acid (AA), arginine (Arg), cysteine (Cys) and lysine (Lys) were obtained from Beijing Biodi Biotech (Beijing, China). The rest of the reagents were higher than analytical grade. A MilliQ purification system was used to produce ultrapure water.

Ninety-six well plates containing the reagents were placed in a Molecular Devices Spectra Max M2 enzyme labeler and assayed to obtain the corresponding fluorescence and UV–Vis spectra. The fluorescence decay lifetime and absolute quantum yield were obtained from the FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the samples were obtained by a Hitachi (H-7650, 80 kV) transmission electron microscope, and Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of the nickel clusters were obtained using a Nicolet 380 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Hydrodynamic dimensions were obtained by Malvern Zetasizer (U.K.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) profiles were determined on a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250XI using a 300 W Xe lamp as a light source with an optical power density of 100 mW/cm2. The ESI-MS spectra were recorded on a hybrid LTQ XL™ mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientifc, Bremen, Germany). The chemical composition of NiNCs was analyzed by using nano-ESI mass spectrometry in negative-ion mode (capillary voltage, −2.1 kV; capillary temperature, 180 °C; m/z range, 300–2000). The samples were prepared in a mixture of methanol and water (50 % by volume) at a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL with ammonia (0.1 % by volume) and directly injected into the mass spectrometer.

All glass instruments were soaked in aqua regia for 24 h and then rinsed with deionized water. NiNCs were synthesized according to the method of literature [45]. Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (3.0 mL, 20 mM) and glutathione (3.0 mL, 50 mM) were added to deionized water (21.0 mL) under vigorously stirring, followed by ultrasonication for 5 min at room temperature. Adjust the pH of the mixture to 10 with NaOH (1.0 M), and then AA (3.0 mL, 112 mM) is added drop by drop to the mixture. Then, it was stirred for 24 h at 90 °C in an oil bath. To further purify the NiNCs, the reaction mixture was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retaining a molecular weight of 1000 Da) in deionized water for 12 h until the pH of the mixture was 7.0. The dialyzed reaction solution was lyophilized to gain the buff solid. The termination product was stored at a temperature of 4 °C in a refrigerator for future utilization.

The influences of Fe3+ concentration (0.50–2.00 mmol/L), pH (3.0–8.0) and reaction time (0–34 min) on the fluorescence burst and resumption were investigated before the detection of AA. Under the optimal experimental conditions, 100 μL NiNCs (1.0 mg/mL), 100 μL Fe3+ (0.50 mmol/L), and acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.0, 40 mmol/L) mixture incubated for 2 min at room temperature. A series of AA standard solutions of different concentrations (0–1000 μmol/L) were added to the NiNCs/Fe3+ mixture, and the mixture was incubated for 30 min at room temperature before recording the fluorescence spectra at the excitation wavelength of 358 nm and emission wavelength of 448 nm. All solutions were obtained by preparing deionized water.

For the sake of studying and evaluating the selectivity of the above assay systems for the detection of AA, the interference of carbohydrates (Glu, Malt and Fru), amino acids (Ser, Arg, Cys, and Lys), metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, and K+) and l-glutathione (GSH) was detected. The concentrations of Cys and Lys were the same as AA at 500 μmol/L, and the concentrations of the remaining substances were 5 times higher than the AA concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical feasibility of the sensing with NiNCs/Fe3+ in real applications was evaluated by determining the AA values of real samples. In this paper, three different vitamin C tablets, fruit beverages and two different fresh fruits are used as actual samples. The extraction of ascorbic acid from vitamin C tablets was based on previous studies [46]. The three tablets were ground into powder and 0.0034 g of the tablet powder was weighed and dissolved in 7 mL of ultrapure water. The pretreatment methods of two type of fruits are as follows: First, the skin and edible part of the fruits are separated, the edible part is mashed into the homogenized paste, and 0.9537 g of oranges and tomatoes homogenate were weighed into two respective centrifuge tubes, and 5.0 mL of ultrapure water was added, and centrifuged for 10 min at 6,000 rpm. The supernatant of the samples was collected. Notably, the filtrate obtained by passing the tablet extracts, fruits supernatant, and beverages were filtered through 0.45 μm filtration membranes. The filtrates of tablet extracts, oranges, tomatoes, and beverages were then diluted separately for the spiked recovery test, and their final dilutions were 4, 5, 10, and 30 times, respectively. The test procedure for AA in samples is the same as for standard samples. The collected detection data (n = 3) were statistically analyzed.

As shown in Fig. 1 A, NiNCs have the presence of UV absorption at 250 nm. The absence of a surface plasma resonance band between 390–450 nm also confirms the successful formation of NiNCs [47]. NiNCs have a maximum excitation wavelength of 358 nm and a maximum emission wavelength of 448 nm, and Stokes shift of 90 nm. The aqueous solution of NiNCs exhibits light-yellow color by the daylight. They fluoresce blue when exposed to UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 1A, inset). From the UV–vis spectra (Fig. S1), the disappearance of the absorption peak at 300 nm of Ni2+ proves the successful synthesis of NiNCs. The absorption peaks of NiNCs at both about 250 nm and 300 nm are decreased significantly after NiNCs is purified by a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1,000 Da (Fig. S2), indicating that the excessive AA and Ni2+ is removed from NiNCs through dialysis. The fluorescence emission spectra under different excitations are scanned (Fig. 1B), and the position of the maximum emission wavelength corresponding to different excitations is not changed, demonstrating that NiNCs are not excitation-dependent and structurally homogeneous. The absolute quantum yield of the prepared NiNCs is estimated to be about 0.85 %, which is measured by the integrating sphere technique with excitation at a wavelength of 358 nm.

As displayed in Fig. 2 A, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images characterize that NiNCs are roughly spherical with lattice distance of 0.23 nm, which is consistent with the results reported [45]. The average hydrodynamic diameter of about 5.5 nm for NiNCs is obtained by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 2B). The surface chemical groups of NiNCs are characterized by using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). In the infrared spectra of NiNCs (Fig. 2C), the characteristic absorption peak is observed at 2525 cm−1 for GSH molecules, which is the stretching vibrational absorption peak of the S–H bond. Meanwhile, this peak disappears in NiNCs, suggesting the cleavage of the S–H bond. So, the GSH molecules are modified on the surface of the NiNCs through the Ni–S bond. The elemental composition and valence states of NiNCs are determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and the XPS survey spectra show that NiNCs contain the elements of Ni, C, N, O, and S (Fig. S3), and two XPS peaks for Ni 2p at 855.33 eV and 873.00 eV in Fig. 2D, corresponding for Ni (0) [31]. This suggests that Ni2+ is completely reduced and NiNCs were successfully synthesized. The above results offer compelling evidence that the water-soluble fluorescent NiNCs have been synthesized.

To further explore the chemical structure information of the purified NiNCs, electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) is used to determine the molecular composition of NiNCs. As shown in Fig. 3 , two distinct peaks, m/z at 1380.91997 and m/z at 1402.87997, are observed in mass spectrum in negative ion mode. One of the peaks m/z at 1402.87997 can be attributed to [Ni3(SG)4-H]- (the inset of Fig. 3), and the isotopic pattern of which are highly consistent with the simulated peaks. Another strong peak m/z at 1380.91997 can be attributed to [Ni2(SG)4+2H2O–H]-, whose isotopic pattern peaks (Fig. S4). The above results demonstrate the existence of two prominent fragments [Ni3(SG)4 and Ni2(SG)4] for the synthesized NiNCs.

To verify the stability of NiNCs, the following measures are performed. As seen in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of NiNCs remained stable in 0–500 mM salt solution. Moreover, NiNCs still retain more than 90 % of the original fluorescence intensity after storage at 4 °C for 30 days (Fig. 4B). These data indicate that NiNCs have excellent stability in storage and in a broad range of the high salt concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design strategy of NiNCs/Fe3+ fluorescent sensor for the detection of AA
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence of NiNCs was significantly quenched by the addition of Fe3+ as shown in Fig. 5 A, while the strong blue fluorescence disappears as seen in the photographs under 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. 5A, inset). On the contrary, the fluorescence of NiNCs is restored by the introduction of AA, which is responsible for the redox effect (Eq. 1) between Fe3+ and AA.
Image 1

Simultaneously, the blue fluorescence is observed again (Fig. 5A, inset). It can be noted that Fe2+ has a slight quenching effect on the fluorescence of NiNCs, but the quenching effect is negligible compared to that of Fe3+ at the same concentration, and AA does not affect the fluorescence of NiNCs alone. (Fig. S5). In order to investigate the mechanism of quenching the fluorescence of NiNCs by Fe3+, we measured the morphologies and particle sizes of the three systems including NiNCs, NiNCs/Fe3+, and NiNCs/Fe3+/AA. TEM images further demonstrated the agglomeration of NiNCs is caused by Fe3+ in Fig. 5B. The aggregated NiNCs/Fe3+ partially disperses after the addition of AA in Fig. 5C. According to the particle size distribution by DLS in Fig. 5D, the average diameter of NiNCs is about 5.50 nm. Notably, the particle size is increased to approximately 211.93 nm after the addition of Fe3+. The fluorescence quenching of the NiNCs may be attributed to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) behavior through the coordination between Fe3+ and carboxyl and amino groups of glutathione on NiNCs surface [44] rather than electrostatic interaction (Fig. S6). More importantly, the particle size is decreased to 41.67 nm after the addition of AA to the NiNCs/Fe3+ system. The absorption peak at 275 nm for NiNCs/AA system is significantly decreased after adding Fe3+ (Fig. S7), which may be due to the redox reaction between AA and Fe3+. Thus, the fluorescence of NiNCs is distinctly restored. What is more, it can be observed that the UV–vis absorption of Fe3+ overlaps with the excitation peaks of NiNCs to a certain extent in Fig. 5E, which proved the existence of the internal filtration effect (IFE). Additionally, there is a small overlap between the UV absorption peak of Fe2+ and the excitation spectrum of NiNCs (Fig. S8). Further, the fluorescence intensity of NiNCs presents imperceptible changes after adding Fe2+ (Fig. S5), while Fe3+ can largely quench the fluorescence of NiNCs (Fig. 5A). Therefore, the effect of Fe2+ on the fluorescence system can be neglected during the detection of AA. In order to further grope the mechanism of this fluorescence quenching effect, we measured time-resolved fluorescence curves. As illustrated in Fig. 5F, the fluorescence lifetime of NiNCs and NiNCs/Fe3+ is 3.95 ns and 3.11 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime did not change significantly with the addition of Fe3+, implying that the process may follow a simple static quenching mechanism possibly because of the formation NiNCs/Fe3+ complex. From the above results, Fe3+ ions initiate the fluorescence quenching of NiNCs by integrating ACQ with IFE properties. This investigation proves the rationality and feasibility of the “on-off-on” fluorescence sensor for AA detection based on NiNCs tailored by Fe3+.

To improve the detection sensitivity of AA, the pH value of the detection system, the concentration of quenching agent Fe3+ and the incubation time were optimized. To investigate the effect of pH on the NiNCs/Fe3+ system, a series of acetic acid-sodium acetate buffers with pH = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 and 8 are added to the NiNCs/Fe3+ system. The fluorescent quenching efficiency (QE, Eq. 2) is used as a selection criterion:
(2)
QE
( % )
=
(
F 0
‐
F 1
)
/
F 0

Where F1 and F0 represent for the fluorescent intensity of NiNCs in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively. As given in Fig. 6 A, the quenching effect of Fe3+ on NiNCs fluorescence is best at pH = 5. Protonated ascorbic acid is the form that is dominant at pH values below 4.25, with a pKa of 4.25, and exhibits high reducing power [48]. Meanwhile, Fe3+ is less stable in alkaline medium as it forms insoluble iron hydroxide and the fluorescent intensity of NiNCs is also weaker in alkaline solution (Fig. S9). The above results indicate that the optimum pH = 5.0 is chosen for subsequent experiments. It can be seen in Fig. 6B that the FL QE increased gradually with the increase of Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0 to 2.0 mmol/L. To the system containing different Fe3+ concentrations, 0.5 mmol/L AA is added separately, and the fluorescent recovery efficient (RE, Eq. 3) of the system after the addition of AA is recorded.
(3)
RE
( % )
= ( F ‐
F 1
) /
(
F 0
‐
F 1
)
Where F0, F1, and F is the fluorescent intensity of NiNCs, NiNCs/Fe3+, and NiNCs/Fe3+/AA for appropriate incubation time in buffer solution, respectively.

The RE values is decreased slowly with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. When the Fe3+ concentration was 0.5 mmol/L, the RE value reached the maximum value of 55.34 % in Fig. 6B. The optimization of Fe3+ concentration in the NiNCs/Fe3+ system is essential to improve the analytical performance. Therefore, the concentration of Fe3+ chosen to detect AA was 0.5 mmol/L. To determine the optimal incubation time for the assay, the change curves of fluorescence intensity with incubation time for the two systems NiNCs/Fe3+ and NiNCs/Fe3+/AA are collected in Fig. 6C. The intensity of NiNCs/Fe3+ is decreased sharply within 2.0 min after the addition of NiNCs at a concentration of 0.5 mmol/L Fe3+ and the fluorescence intensity is basically stable after 2.0 min. When 0.5 mmol/L AA is added to the NiNCs/Fe3+ system, the fluorescence intensity of the system is increased at a high rate within 8.0 min, but slowed down from 8.0 to 28 min, and the fluorescence intensity is gradually stabilized after 30 min of the reaction, which demonstrates that the reaction time for 30 min is sufficient for AA to reduce Fe3+ and, allowing the system to reinstate fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the reaction time of 2.0 min for NiNCs/Fe3+ and 30 min fluorescence recovery time for NiNCs/Fe3+/AA are selected for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity testing for AA detection
ce:section.ce:para: AA at different concentrations (0–1000 μmol/L) are added to the NiNCs/Fe3+ system under optimal conditions to study the changes in fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity and the ratio value of F/F0 (F0 and F represent for the fluorescence intensity of NiNCs and NiNCs/Fe3+/AA, respectively.) of the whole system is gradually increased with the increase of AA concentration in Fig. 7 A and B. When the AA concentration was in the range of 20–200 μmol/L, the ratio of F to F0 shows a good linear relationship with its concentration, and the correlation coefficient in Fig. 7C is R2 = 0.9904. The linear equation is F/F0 = 0.0021c + 0.2145 with LOD of 7.45 μmol/L according to the 3σ/k rule. The detection limits of this method are lower or comparable to those of other reported electrochemical, colorimetric, and fluorescent sensors for AA detection listed in Table S1.

Some metal ions, amino acids, carbohydrates, and common reducing substances were selected to test the selectivity of the NiNCs/Fe3+ system, and the fluorescence recovery of the system by interfering substances was further evaluated to assess the selectivity of NiNCs/Fe3+ in the AA assay. As shown in Fig. 7D, the bio-reductive substances glutathione, cysteine and lysine show slight fluorescence recovery, and the other interfering substances offer only particularly weak fluorescence recovery, whereas AA exhibits strong fluorescence recovery on NiNCs/Fe3+. These results indicate that the method is highly selective for the detection of AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AA content in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of assessing the practical application of the NiNCs/Fe3+ system in actual samples, we chose to determine the AA content in three different vitamin C tablets, two different vitamin beverages, and two different fresh fruits. The measured AA contents from the three brands of vitamin C tablets from and two brands of vitamin beverages are generally consistent with their specified contents in Fig. 8 . AA content was also measured in small tomatoes and oranges (Fig. S10). In order to further investigate the accuracy and reliability of the sensor in detecting AA, spiked recovery experiments were carried out on two brands of vitamin C beverages and two types of fruits as mentioned above. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries range from 96.23 % to 109.92 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs, n = 3) are less than 2.4 %, which indicate that the sensor has high accuracy and reliability in detecting the AA content of the real samples.

In summary, an on-off-on switchable fluorescence approach has been designed for the detection of AA based on water-soluble NiNCs with the assistance of Fe3+. The fluorescence of NiNCs is quenched significantly after adding Fe3+ by combining aggregation-caused quenching with internal filtering effect mechanism. And then, the fluorescence of NiNCs/Fe3+ was effectively restored by AA on the basis of redox reactions. This sensing system can be applied to the detection of AA in commercial tablets and beverages samples with high accuracy and sensitivity. The good recovery results are obtained in the beverages and fresh fruits. The proposed sensor exhibits distinct advantages including ease-of-operation, environmental-friendliness, cost-effectiveness, and good stability. What's more, our work opens an avenue for the extension of NiNCs in food analysis.

Jiaqing Li: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Nan Yao: Investigation, Formal analysis, Formal analysis. Xiwen Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Yueying Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21775087) and Scientific Research Project of Beijing Educational Committee (Grant No. KM202010028007).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluation of the effects of ascorbic acid on metabolism of human mesenchymal stem cells
Stem Cell Res Ther

Trends Food Sci Technol

Am J Clin Nutr

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ascorbic acid in cancer treatment: let the phoenix fly
